trajectory_summary: The call opened with genuine bill pain and a trust gift (disability disclosure) that gave Bucky two high-leverage moments. He converted neither. The pitch came too fast after the $800 anchor, the credibility challenges were answered with bare assertion, the vulnerability disclosure received no empathy or value bridge, and the Monday callback was accepted without any button-up structure. Wilfred's closing commitment ('I'm getting ready to call them anyway') upgrades the lead quality — this is not a ghost — but the appointment is structurally fragile: no time locked, wife not included, no computer/no-driving anchor, no verbal handshake.
outcome_dispute: If v1 tagged this as 'booked,' reclassify as callback_unstructured. Bucky said 'I'll call you Monday' with no locked time, no wife included, no button-up elements completed. That is a soft callback, not a sit-eligible appointment. The distinction matters for comp: setters are paid on sits, not callbacks.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: we have everything we need, and I it'll take me a second to work on your design. So I can get that, like, ready by Monday, and I can have somebody call you.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Bucky accepted a Monday deferral and completed zero of the six required button-up elements — no time locked, wife excluded, no verbal handshake. Wilfred's closing commitment at 05:07 confirms this lead has genuine intent. The structural fragility is entirely Bucky's to fix. On a paid lead, an unstructured callback that doesn't convert to a sit pays nothing. This is a direct comp-protection issue, not just a coaching note.

key_moments.quote: I started paying on it, but, man, that's a lot of money, bro.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-energy moment. Wilfred is animated, using informal language ('bro'), and volunteering frustration unprompted. The schema requires the bill to be captured AND flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Bucky acknowledged ('Oh, yeah. That is a lot of money') and immediately pivoted to the program pitch. The flip — turning the $800 into the enemy and solar into the replacement — never happened. Capture without flip is the universal failure mode the schema flags.

key_moments.quote: I'm gonna call them after I get off the phone with you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Wilfred is so activated by his bill that he's planning a utility call immediately after this one. This is a forward-action signal — he is not passive about his bill pain. Bucky walked past it entirely. This line was the perfect setup for the objection_inversion flip: 'That call is exactly why we should talk — because whatever they tell you, the bill isn't going away. Solar replaces it.' Instead, Bucky said 'Okay' and moved on.

key_moments.quote: And what what is the average bill?
key_moments.stewart_read: Wilfred is asking a direct curiosity question — he wants to know what the solar bill would look like. This is the highest-value question a prospect can ask at this stage. Bucky's response ('It's it's it's dependent on your home and what you're paying now. Like, you would see crazy safe.') is incomplete, trails off, and fails to redirect to the design process as the proof mechanism. The schema's curiosity_over_features principle says the setter should make customers curious to see their specific math — not answer the question with a vague savings claim. The correct move was to use Wilfred's $800 as the anchor and redirect: 'That's exactly what the design shows you — based on your $800, here's what we'd replace it with. That's why we need to get you in front of the specialist.'

Wilfred asked 'Are you sure?' after the bill-swap pitch. Bucky responded with a bare assertion of certainty — 'I'm definite' — with no grounding. The schema's numeric_discipline rule prohibits ungrounded savings claims. 'I'm definite' is the verbal equivalent of a fabricated number: it asserts certainty the setter cannot back up at this stage. The correct move was to redirect to the design process as the proof mechanism — 'The design is what shows you the exact numbers; that's why we build it first.'
